description: Court cases and precedents. These cards fire when the judiciary exists and a legal dispute requires resolution.

cards.title: The First Case — Personhood and Property
cards.description: A merchant claims his ship has legal personhood. The Treasury wants to tax it as an actor. The Scriptorium wants to classify it as property. The new Codex Court must rule.

choices.text: The ship is property. Personhood requires birth or naturalization.
Derivative standing — rights through the owner's contracts.
The Court cannot rule. The Assembly must legislate.

cards.title: The Status Freeze Doctrine
cards.description: The Court formalizes its power to freeze disputes. How broad should the freeze be?

choices.text: Limited freeze — one year, renewable by Assembly.
No freeze power. Court advises; Assembly decides.

cards.title: The Imperator Compromise Ruling
cards.description: The Court must rule on whether the legislature can create an executive office with temporary emergency powers.

choices.text: Yes. The Imperator is constitutional. Limited term, defined powers.
Yes, but only during declared emergencies.
No. The Assembly cannot delegate its sovereignty.

cards.title: The Judicial Review Question
cards.description: A Federalist proposal would grant the Court power to strike down any legislation it finds 'legally incoherent.' Corwin's dream — or nightmare.

choices.text: Grant judicial review. The Court is supreme.
Limited review. Only on constitutional questions.
Reject. Ipso facto oreo — the Court cannot expand itself.

cards.title: The Rights of the Unclassified
cards.description: A class-action suit argues that processing delays violate the principle that no person within the Bay may be legally invisible.

choices.text: The Civitas Act is constitutional. Delays must be resolved by the Assembly.
Delays are unconstitutional. The Assembly must act within 60 days.
All unclassified persons are granted provisional status immediately.
